sections.heading: Guidance areas
sections.normalized_text: Guidance areas All Guidance Knowledge, skills and performance Safety and quality Communication, partnership and teamwork Maintaining trust Search As well as searching, you can browse the Guidance . Search

sections.heading: Key points
heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
sections.normalized_text: You should refer a patient presenting with flashes and/or floaters to a colleague if you do not feel competent to manage the patient. You should follow local protocols for management and referral of these patients. A285 This Guidance does not change what you must do under the law.

sections.heading: Principles of examining a patient who presents with flashes and floaters
heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
Principles of examining a patient who presents with flashes and floaters
sections.normalized_text: A286 If you are unable to carry out an adequate examination when you examine a patient who presents with flashes and/or floaters, you must refer the patient to a practitioner who is competent to do this. A287 You should ensure that front line or support staff are trained to deal with such a patient who contacts the practice. Patients should be told a diagnosis cannot be reached without an examination. A288 If you carry out an examination, you should continue until you detect a problem and can make a diagnosis or have sufficient evidence to decide what action to take. A289 If you suspect a retinal break or tear, you should, as a minimum: take a detailed history and symptoms, looking for particular risk factors examine the anterior vitreous to look for pigment cells perform a dilated fundal examination, using an indirect viewing technique give appropriate advice to the patient, which you back up with written information. A290 You should follow local protocols for the management and referral of these patients. A291 You should keep full and accurate records of all patient contact. See section on Patient records .

sections.heading: Referral
heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
sections.normalized_text: A292 The majority of patients presenting with flashes and/or floaters will not have a retinal detachment. If you do not feel competent to manage a patient presenting with flashes and/or floaters, you should refer them to an appropriate colleague. Emergency referrals include: retinal detachment pigment in the anterior vitreous (tobacco dust) vitreous, retinal or pre-retinal haemorrhage lattice degeneration or retinal break, with symptoms. A293 A retinal hole or tear does not always lead to retinal detachment. However, you should refer the patient if they are having relevant symptoms and any of the signs in section Principles of examining a patient with symptoms of flashes and floaters are present. See section on Working with colleagues .

sections.heading: Managing the patient
heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
sections.normalized_text: A294 Most cases of floaters are due to posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) or vitreous degeneration. You can manage a patient in your practice if you confirm they have a PVD after dilated ocular examination and: vision is unchanged no retinal tear or detachment is present no pigment is present in the anterior vitreous the patient is well informed about what symptoms to expect if the retina does break or detach subsequently you issue the patient with written information to support your diagnosis and advice.

heading_path: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters

Principles of examining a patient who presents with flashes and floaters A286 If you are unable to carry out an adequate examination when you examine a patient who presents with flashes and/or floaters, you must refer the patient to a practitioner who is competent to do this. A287 You should ensure that front line or support staff are trained to deal with such a patient who contacts the practice. A288 If you carry out an examination, you should continue until you detect a problem and can make a diagnosis or have sufficient evidence to decide what action to take. Referral A292 The majority of patients presenting with flashes and/or floaters will not have a retinal detachment. If you do not feel competent to manage a patient presenting with flashes and/or floaters, you should refer them to an appropriate colleague.
